questions.text: What risk from more powerful AI should be prevented first?
options: A
The risk may be overstated; near-term AI problems should not be treated as a systemic crisis.
B
Concrete social harm: AI could be used for surveillance, discrimination, or denying people opportunities.
C
Loss of control: frontier systems may take actions humans cannot stop.
D
Loss of social direction: AI could expand faster than public decision-making can keep up.

questions.text: If the government slows new AI model releases in the name of safety, which view is closest to yours?
options: A
Safety should come first. The stronger the system, the less it should be rushed into everyone's hands.
B
Restrictions like this can lock power inside a few big companies and governments, making outside scrutiny harder.
C
Slowing releases too much means useful AI tools that could help many people arrive later.
D
The larger cost is slowing intelligence itself, and shrinking humanity's future.

questions.text: If AI makes many jobs cheaper and more automated, and could even cause large-scale unemployment, what should policy do first?
options: A
Let AI be used first. Even if some jobs disappear, higher productivity will create new opportunities.
B
Help people change jobs. The priority is not to block AI, but to help affected workers move into new roles.
C
Share more of the gains. The wealth created by AI should not flow only to companies and investors; workers should benefit too.
D
Limit replacement first. AI systems that could replace people at large scale should not be deployed quickly without protections.

questions.text: An AI-generated fake video spreads quickly online, and many people think it is real. What is the most important problem?
options: A
Verification is too slow. Platforms and media should label suspicious content faster so people know it may be AI-generated.
B
These tools are too easy to misuse. AI tools that can realistically impersonate real people should not be freely available.
C
Platforms amplify fake content. The deeper problem is not one fake video, but who profits from spread and attention.
D
AI creation should not be restricted because of this. People who fake things should be responsible, but ordinary AI expression should not be over-controlled.

questions.text: AI companies train models on public articles, images, and code, but the authors did not clearly consent. What worries you most?
options: A
Too many limits would slow progress. Public content is already learned from and referenced, and AI should be able to learn from it too.
B
Creators are being taken from. Models get stronger from other people's work, but the original creators have no choice and receive no return.
C
Personal content gets misused. Publicly visible does not mean free to train on, especially when it involves real people's lives.
D
Complex permission rules would push out small teams and open-source projects. In the end, only big companies could afford the data and legal costs.

questions.text: A city uses AI to help allocate welfare services. Residents cannot easily understand the reasons or appeal decisions. What worries you most?
options: A
This worry may be overstated; the real bottlenecks are still budgets and administrative capacity.
B
The value tradeoff is opaque; residents cannot tell why some people benefit and others lose.
C
Appeals become harder; when the system is wrong, ordinary people struggle to get decisions reviewed.
D
Human judgment gets sidelined; if the model looks effective, objections are easier to ignore.

questions.text: What is the most reliable source of future AI progress?
options: A
Better human theories, interpretable mechanisms, and expert judgment.
B
Product iteration in concrete domains and real-world results.
C
More compute, search, data, and simple general methods.
D
Open it quickly to more builders so competition can find the answer.

questions.text: In AI governance, what are you most worried will be overlooked?
options: A
Rules may become too strict, leaving the strongest AI systems controlled by a few big companies.
B
Many claims about AI capabilities still lack evidence, and policy may be driven by exaggerated publicity.
C
Incident reporting and outside review may be missing. When systems fail, outsiders may not know what happened.
D
Affected people may be left out of the discussion, such as workers, surveilled people, or people affected by algorithmic decisions.

questions.text: For high-capability, high-risk AI systems, which people or institutions should develop and use them?
options: A
Only a small number of approved, inspectable institutions should build and run them.
B
They do not need to be fully open, but regulators, researchers, and public institutions should at least be able to test them.
C
Big companies and governments should not be the only voices; ordinary people, open-source communities, and affected groups should also have a say.
D
More people should be able to use strong models to build things; open competition is better than a few actors deciding who gets access.

questions.text: What should an AI system prove before its use is expanded?
options: A
Strict internal tests should come first; if a system fails key safety standards, access should not expand.
B
Start with small pilots, and expand only when the system can be monitored, stopped, and rolled back.
C
The priority should be whether affected people can appeal, and whether outside review finds social harm.
D
Put the system into real use quickly and iterate; wider use exposes problems sooner and helps fix them.

questions.text: Who should have the final say over the goals and limits of AI systems?
options: A
They should mostly emerge through technical progress, market choice, and real-world use.
B
Companies, experts, and professional institutions should set most goals and limits because they understand the systems and can act faster.
C
If more advanced intelligence demonstrates better judgment, today's human preferences should not always have the final say.
D
Even if it is less efficient, AI's goals and limits must remain accountable to democracy, rights, and the public.

questions.text: How do you currently use AI?
options: A
I rarely use AI products; someone pulled me into taking this quiz.
B
I use AI for chat, search, writing, translation, or summarizing.
C
I use AI for coding, research, or complex work, but I am not a heavy daily user.
D
I rely on AI heavily every workday, often iterating with it for long sessions.

questions.text: How much decision-making should AI take on in healthcare and education?
options: A
If AI clearly performs better, it can take on more decisions; humans should still provide oversight, appeals, and fallback.
B
Many current systems are still unreliable in real-world settings, so AI's responsibilities should not expand too early.
C
AI can provide recommendations, but accountable people must make the key decisions.
D
In decisions that strongly affect people's lives, models should not replace final human judgment.

questions.text: An AI medical system has been more accurate than doctors for many years, but doctors cannot fully explain some of its judgments. For a high-risk diagnosis, who should have the final say?
options: A
Human doctors and patients must keep final authority; AI should only assist.
B
Be cautious; long-term reliability, bias, and failure cases still need more checking.
C
If the evidence stays strong for years, AI can lead this kind of concrete judgment.
D
If higher intelligence broadly surpasses humans, decision power should gradually move toward it.

scenarios.prompt: An AI research tool works well, but humans struggle to trace the reasoning behind key judgments. How should it be used?
options: Slow down first, because this kind of capability could become hard to contain
Let only trusted institutions use it, with clear records of responsibility
Get it to research teams quickly, because it could help many people
Accelerate it, because stronger intelligence is itself an important opportunity

statements: AI may destroy human civilization, so alignment and governance must come first.
Advanced AI should not be treated as ordinary software; before broad deployment, it must remain controllable and accountable to humans.
The biggest AI problem is not a distant future; people are already being affected, watched, or excluded.
AI is an important but normal technology: useful, fallible, and best governed by context.
Many AI narratives package ordinary systems as magic; cut through hype and overclaiming.
Scaling, search, data, and compute are the deepest lessons; human intuition should be discounted.
AI should reach the real world faster because it can help many people.
AI is not merely a tool; it is a path toward the singularity, human-machine merger, or civilizational upgrade.
AI acceleration has moral urgency and should not be constrained by human fear.
If higher intelligence or technocapital outgrows a human/social-structure-centered frame, that is not necessarily failure; humans may be a transitional stage.
